title: Electropolymerization of Tetrakis(o-aminophenyl)porphyrin and Relevant Transition Metal Complexes from Aqueous Solution. The Resulting Modified Electrodes as Potentiometric Sensors
abstract:
Electropolymerization of Tetrakis(o-aminophenyl)porphyrin
and Relevant Transition Metal Complexes from Aqueous
Solution. The Resulting Modi®ed Electrodes as Potentiometric
Sensors
Enrico Maria Bruti, Marco Giannetto, Giovanni Mori,* and Renato Seeber
 Dipartimento di Chimica Generale ed Inorganica, Chimica Analitica, Chimica Fisica, UniversitaÁ di Parma, Parco Area delle Scienze 17a, I- 43100
Parma, Italy
Dipartimento di Chimica, UniversitaÁ di Modena, Via G.Campi, 183, I±41100 Modena, Italy
Received: July 24, 1998
Final version: March 12, 1999
Abstract
The mechanism of electrogeneration of polyporphyrin from ortho-substituted phenylporphyrins, either free or complexing transition metals,
is different when performed in aqueous solution from that operative in organic solvents, that has been reported in the literature. Poly-
merization occurs through the amino phenyl substituents, leading to a three dimensional `cage' structure. The polymer is sensitive to
morphology and nature of the surface it grows on. The mass deposited on a gold electrode was about 2  10ÿ4 g=cm2. If the polymerization
is performed under potentiodynamic conditions, the polymer forms essentially during the ®rst scan, while no signi®cant further growth is
observed in subsequent potential cycles, from both free and metallated porphyrins. All polyporphyrin ®lms, except the one obtained from
CoTAPP, exhibit poor electrical conductivity, due to the three dimensional growth of the polymer chain that does not allow the necessary
conjugation of  orbitals inside the macromolecules. On the other hand, interesting properties are shown by the unmetallated as well as by
metallated polymer ®lms as possible coverages for `coated wire' ion selective electrodes. The results obtained with respect to different ions
and, in particular, a selectivity study carried out on iodide ions are reported.
Keywords: Polymer modi®ed electrodes, Polyporphyrins, Potentiometric sensors
1. Introduction
Electrodes coated with electroactive polymers have been
intensively studied over the past two decades [1, 2]. Electroactive
polymers mainly include those based on aniline, phenol, pyrrole,
and thiophene chains. In particular, aniline-substituted porphyr-
ins and derivatives can be electropolymerized [3±5] in order to
obtain surface modi®ed electrodes with the aim of realizing
potentiometric or amperometric sensors.
In recent years, ion selective electrodes (ISEs) based on
metalloporphyrins have been widely used. Co(III), Sn(IV),
Mn(III) and Mo(IV) porphyrin complexes included inside PVC
membranes have shown selectivity towards speci®c anions [6].
Selectivity is also affected by the nature of substituent groups on
the ring. Long lifetime ISEs are obtained by electropolymerization
on platinum and glassy carbon electrodes. Furthermore, poten-
tiometric sensors obtained by electrochemical polymerization of
tetra(o-aminophenyl)porphyrins have been realized [7±10].
Though lot of works on electrochemical polymerization of
similar cyclic compounds has been published, only a few articles
dealing with the possible underlying mechanism can be found.
The aim of this article is to report on electropolymerization of
tetra(o-aminophenyl)porphyrins and of the corresponding cobalt,
zinc, and nickel complexes in aqueous media and to advance
hypotheses
on
the
relevant
mechanism.
Furthermore,
the
potentiometric
responses
of
the
resulting
chemically
modi®ed electrodes with respect to a series of ionic species are
examined.
2. Experimental
Tetra(o-aminophenyl)porphyrin (TAPP) (1) and the relevant
metal complexes with cobalt(II), zinc(II) and nickel(II) ions were
synthesized using the method described by Murray et al. [3].
Electropolymerizations were performed using the computer-
ized Autolab PGSTAT 20 electrochemical instrument (Eco-
chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) connected to a 663 VA Stand
(Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland). The three-electrode electro-
chemical cell was equipped with a platinum counter electrode
and a AgjAgCl reference electrode. Working electrodes of dif-
ferent materials have been used, i.e., platinum, glassy carbon
(GC), gold, and rhodium. This last electrode was obtained by
electrodepositing rhodium metal on a gold electrode polarized for
ca. 5 min at a potential of ÿ0.30 V, inside a 1 M rhodium(III)
sulfate aqueous solution. Electropolymerizations were accom-
plished using 1 M or 0.1 M HCl aqueous solutions containing
1 mM porphyrin or 1 mM metalloporphyrin monomer, by cycling
the potential between the limits  0.2 V and  1.2 V, at a
potential scan rate of 50 mV=s. The working platinum electrode
was previously polished with 0.3 and 0.05 mm alumina powder,
then rinsed with distilled water and sonicated in an ultrasonic
bath for 5 min. Potentiostatic measurements were carried out on a
platinum rotating disk electrode (Tacussel, Lyon, France) with an
electrochemical area of 0.74 cm2.
Electropolymerizations were also carried out on Indium-Tin
Oxide (ITO) glass and on gold-coated quartz electrodes, on
which UV-visible analyses were performed. A Uvikon 860
Electroanalysis 1999, 11, No. 8
# WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
1040±0397/99/0806±0565 $17.50:50=0
565
 spectrophotometer (Kontron Instrument, Zurich, Switzerland)
has been employed. Electrochemical impedance data on the
coating ®lm [4, 5] were obtained using the same Autolab
instrument, equipped with the proper FRA module and software.
The measurements were performed on a polymer-coated plati-
num electrode using different electrolytes, scanning the fre-
quency between 10 KHz and 0.1 Hz (rms : 0.01 V). FT-IR spectra
were recorded with a Nicolet 5PC FT-IR instrument (Nicolet,
Wisconsin). The surface polymer deposit morphology was
investigated by a NANOSCOPE IIIA (Digital Instruments Inc.,
Santa Barbara, CA) Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) operating
in contact mode. In situ deposit weight vs. potential measure-
ments were performed using an Electrochemical Quartz Crystal
Microbalance (EQCM) [11], connected with a Fluke Model
PM6685 Universal Frequency Counter.
Potentiometric
measurements
were
carried
out
using
a
Radiometer PHM84 digital potentiometer=pHmeter. All tests
were performed at 25 C, using a 50 mL thermostatic cell. A
Te¯on cell was employed when using ¯uoride. The same working
electrode used for the electropolymerization was simply trans-
ferred, after vigorous washing with distilled water, from the
monomer solution into the potentiometric measurement solution.
The reference electrode was a homemade AgjAgCl, KCl(3M)
electrode, separated from the test solutions by a Te¯on porous
disk when working in the presence of ¯uoride ions. Calibrations
were performed in 50 mL solutions buffered at pH 6.00 with
NaH2PO4 0.1 M=NaOH, to which the analyte was added by a
micropipette (TREFF-PIPETTE). The investigated analyte con-
centration
range
was
from
10ÿ6
to
10ÿ1 M.
Sensitivity
(mV=pIon) and detection limit values have been calculated with a
TAPP modi®ed electrode for any halide considered. Further
investigations with metal porphyrin ®lm modi®ed electrode have
been performed on SCNÿ, ClOÿ
4 , NOÿ
3 and NOÿ
2 . Experimental
details on potentiometric selectivity determinations are given in
[12].
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Film Formation and Characterization
3.1.1. Electrochemical Polymerization
Polymerization of free TAPP takes place successfully only at a
potential more positive than  1.10 V vs. AgjAgCl, KCl (3M) in
HCl aqueous solution. No ®lm formation is observed when using
HClO4 or H2SO4 instead. In the very ®rst voltammetric sweep
only two anodic peaks are recorded at  0.89 V and  1.03 V,
respectively. Both these peaks are no longer detectable in the
subsequent scans and look completely similar when using a GC
electrode (see Fig. 1, peaks G and G0). Voltammograms (see Fig.
2) obtained on Pt electrode after more than 5 subsequent
potential scans show three main peaks: two anodic peaks, A,
with  1.12 V and D, with Ep value of  0.65 V, to which a
backward anodic peak, C with Ep  0:55, V is directly asso-
ciated. An additional anodic signal, merged into B, is ascribed to
the oxidation of chloride ions to molecular chlorine. The anodic
peak A only arises in the correspondence to the 5th or 6th
potential scan and progressively increases in height up to about
the 15th sweep. A strongly adherent, thin, gold-colored ®lm
forms on the electrode just after the ®rst scan. This ®lm is
insoluble in the usual inorganic acids, but soluble in acetonitrile
and acetone, and only partially soluble in methanol. Different
peak shapes and locations on the potential axis are observed on
Au and on Rh electrodes, respectively (see Figs. 3 and 4).
Quite different voltammetric curves are recorded during
electropolymerization of metal-porphyrin complexes, depending
on the nature of the metal, i.e., Co, Zn, or Ni. In any case, the
voltammograms show a single anodic peak with Ep  1:05 V
on Pt electrode. The color of the ®lm changes with the metal ion:
that resulting from ZnTAPP is dark-red to gold colored, that from
NiTAPP is gold colored, exhibiting light-blue re¯ections, while
the ®lm from CoTAPP is light-red.
IR analyses by KBr pellets technique have been performed
scraping mechanically with KBr salt ®lm coated Pt rod electro-
des. The NH2 stretching absorptions, present in the monomer
spectrum in the 3200±3600 cmÿ1 wavenumber region, (Fig. 5a),
even if strongly reduced, are still present in the polymer spec-
trum, con®rming that TAPP polymerizes through the aniline
groups in a regioregular `head to tail' addition mechanism [13,
14]. In the region between 1600 and 400 cmÿ1 (Fig. 5b), strong
CN (1614 cmÿ1) and CC (1500±1450 cmÿ1) stretching
absorptions are detectable for both monomer and polymer, while
the CÿH bending and CÿC stretching absorptions of mono-
mer's aniline units lie at 750 and 965 cmÿ1, respectively. The
spectrum relevant to the polymer shows exclusively a broader
CN stretching absorption, due to several CN units of anilinic
bridges, and the CC stretching absorption (1384 cmÿ1). CÿC
stretching and CÿH bending of aniline groups are not detect-
able owing to heavy modi®cation of the aromatic rings involved
in the polymerization.
Due to the steric hindrance of the porphyrin ring [15], it is
reasonable that the polymer chain does not grow at anyone of the
four aniline groups, but only at two of them, opposite from each
other with respect to the macrocyclic ring. The aniline units lie
orthogonally in the polyaniline chains, with a diedhral angle of
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammograms recorded on a 10ÿ3 M TAPP, 1 M HCl
aqueous solution, on a GC electrode. Anodic forward potential scan.
100 mV=s potential scan rate. Peaks G and G0 are recorded in the very
®rst sweep.
Fig. 2. Cyclic voltammograms recorded with a Pt working electrode. 5th
to 15th scans. Other experimental conditions as in Figure 1.

HCl aqueous solution. No ®lm formation is observed when using
HClO4 or H2SO4 instead. In the very ®rst voltammetric sweep
only two anodic peaks are recorded at  0.89 V and  1.03 V,
respectively. Both these peaks are no longer detectable in the
subsequent scans and look completely similar when using a GC
electrode (see Fig. 1, peaks G and G0). Voltammograms (see Fig.
2) obtained on Pt electrode after more than 5 subsequent
potential scans show three main peaks: two anodic peaks, A,
with  1.12 V and D, with Ep value of  0.65 V, to which a
backward anodic peak, C with Ep  0:55, V is directly asso-
ciated. An additional anodic signal, merged into B, is ascribed to
the oxidation of chloride ions to molecular chlorine. The anodic
peak A only arises in the correspondence to the 5th or 6th
potential scan and progressively increases in height up to about
the 15th sweep. A strongly adherent, thin, gold-colored ®lm
forms on the electrode just after the ®rst scan. This ®lm is
insoluble in the usual inorganic acids, but soluble in acetonitrile
and acetone, and only partially soluble in methanol. Different
peak shapes and locations on the potential axis are observed on
Au and on Rh electrodes, respectively (see Figs. 3 and 4).

electropolymerization of metal-porphyrin complexes, depending
on the nature of the metal, i.e., Co, Zn, or Ni. In any case, the
voltammograms show a single anodic peak with Ep  1:05 V
on Pt electrode. The color of the ®lm changes with the metal ion:
that resulting from ZnTAPP is dark-red to gold colored, that from
NiTAPP is gold colored, exhibiting light-blue re¯ections, while
the ®lm from CoTAPP is light-red.

scraping mechanically with KBr salt ®lm coated Pt rod electro-
des. The NH2 stretching absorptions, present in the monomer
spectrum in the 3200±3600 cmÿ1 wavenumber region, (Fig. 5a),
even if strongly reduced, are still present in the polymer spec-
trum, con®rming that TAPP polymerizes through the aniline
groups in a regioregular `head to tail' addition mechanism [13,
14]. In the region between 1600 and 400 cmÿ1 (Fig. 5b), strong
CN (1614 cmÿ1) and CC (1500±1450 cmÿ1) stretching
absorptions are detectable for both monomer and polymer, while
the CÿH bending and CÿC stretching absorptions of mono-

reasonable that the polymer chain does not grow at anyone of the
four aniline groups, but only at two of them, opposite from each
other with respect to the macrocyclic ring. The aniline units lie
orthogonally in the polyaniline chains, with a diedhral angle of

100 mV=s potential scan rate. Peaks G and G0 are recorded in the very

120 due to the CÿNC moieties. Hence, the porphyrin groups,
owing to the steric hindrance, should lie orthogonal to the aniline
they are bounded to. Moreover, the vectors connecting the bar-
icenters of anilines to those of the linked porphyrins, apart from
the diedhral angle with the plane of anilines, should roughly be
helicoidally arranged along the polyanliline chains. A sketch of
the resulting structural unit assembly is reported in Figure 6.
3.1.2. Potentiostatic Measurements
Polymerizations of TAPP at stationary and rotating disk Pt
electrodes have been carried out potentiostatically at  1.12 V.
The amounts of charge spent in eight repeated tests performed
under the same experimental conditions resulted equal to
0:68  0:05 mC cmÿ2 for the rotating disk electrode, allowing
the evaluation, through a Student t-test, of a level of signi®cance
lower than 0.1 % for the difference between the two data samples.
Furthermore, no difference in the appearance of the ®lms grown
using the two methods could be evidenced.
3.1.3. Electrochemical Polymerization on ITO Glass and on
Voltammograms performed on ITO glass only show an anodic
peak with Ep  1:12 V in the ®rst scan. No peak at all is
observed in the subsequent scans. UV-visible spectra performed
on ®lm coated ITO glass after a single potential cycle show a
shift in the typical Soret band from 415 nm in the monomer to
426 nm, due to small deviation from planarity of the porphyrin
ring, and only one Q band (at 519 nm) of the four present in the
monomer spectra, because of the decreased conjugation [16, 17].
Spectra obtained on the ®lm deposited in the ®rst cycle on a gold-
quartz electrode only show a shift of the frequency absorptions,
which are now located at 422 and 510 nm, respectively. Cycling
further the potential does not affect signi®cantly the absorbances
measured, suggesting that no additional deposit growth takes
place. The absorbance data are summarized in Table 1.
3.1.4. Atomic Force Microscopy Analysis
Surface analyses show a polymer ®lm strongly stuck on the Pt
electrode ( Fig. 7). Film thickness measurements, performed with
the AFM instrument software, lead to a mean value of 30 nm,
with thin spikes up to 300 nm, which seems to indicate that some
chains of the polymer grow perpendicularly to the electrode
surface. Completely similar values are obtained after a single
potential cycle as well as after as many as 50 scans.
3.1.5. Frequency Response Analysis
Information on solution resistance, electrode-solution double
layer capacitance, and polymer ®lm characteristics have been
collected by FRA technique. Analyses have been made at dif-
ferent potentials in different aqueous media on electrodes coated
in both NaCl and HCl solutions. The results were plotted in form
of Nyquist diagrams and typical plots are collected in Figure 8.
At variance with the simple situation of a single Pt=solution
interface, in the more complex situation at a Pt=polymer
®lm=electrolyte solution system two phase boundaries have to be
considered. The box A in Figure 8 shows an electric circuit
whose responses ®t those of the modi®ed electrodes in the stu-
died solutions when the values for resistances and capacitances
are those reported in Table 2. Rs is related to the resistance of the
solution, the RC circuit on the left of the drawing accounts for the
Fig. 3. Cyclic voltammograms obtained on Au electrode. Other
experimental conditions as in Figure 1.
Fig. 4. Cyclic voltammograms obtained on Rh electrode. Other
experimental conditions as in Figure 1.
Fig. 5. IR spectra of the polyTAPP ®lm (trace 1) and monomer (trace 2).
a) and b) are relevant to different typical wavenumber ranges.
Electropolymerization of Tetrakis(o-aminophenyl)porphyrin
electrode=polymer ®lm interface, while the contribution to the
overall response attributable to the polymer ®lm(solution inter-
face is accounted for by the RC circuit on the right. Rp0 is the
charge transfer resistance between polymer and electrolyte
solution and Cp is the capacitance of the relevant double layer; W
is the Warburg impedance, giving account of the migration-dif-
fusion phenomena occurring as a consequence of the positive
charging of the polymer ®lm, i.e., of the anionic counter ions in
The Nyquist diagrams drawn out on the basis of data collected
in 1 M Na2SO4 and in 1 M H2SO4, respectively, allow the com-
putation of a very high ®lm resistance. The Warburg impedance
is evidenced in the low frequency portion of the diagrams relative
to measurements performed at  0.2 and  1.12 V, i.e., at lower
or higher potentials with respect to equilibrium, respectively. It is
attributable to H and Clÿ migration into the ®lm, at the lower
and at the higher potential, respectively. In the diagrams obtained
from measurements performed both in 1 M NaCl and in 1 M HCl,
double half circles are present, attributable to the interaction of
chloride ion with either platinum surface (circle at higher fre-
quencies) or with the polymeric surface (circle at lower frquen-
cies): the former one is assumed to be due to electric properties of
the electrode-®lm interface and the latter one to the ®lm-solution
interface. In HCl solution adsorption and corrosion phenomena
are operative at high potential values[18±21]. Evolving chlorine
is supposed to corrode the ®lm surface and to diffuse into it,
causing irreversible polymer chain damage. The impedance
analyses of the ®lms from NiTAPP and ZnTAPP (Fig. 9) do not
show a behavior signi®cantly different from that of the polymer
from TAPP (see Fig. 8), apart from the higher impedance values.
Different results are obtained when working on ®lms obtained
from CoTAPP. At the equilibrium potential (  0.57 V) the
polymer shows a conductive character, as shown by FRA
experiments. Electron transport is made possible by overlapping
between polyaniline bridge orbitals and metal d orbitals [22].
Performing impedance measurements at potentials either well
lower or well higher than the equilibrium value, i.e., at  0.20
and  1.20 V, respectively, an increase of the deposit resistance is
observed. This trend can be explained considering that cobalt ion
can assume 3  as well as 2  oxidation states; while at the
equilibrium potential the charge percolation through the polymer
occurs by electron hopping mechanism involving the cobal-
t(III)=cobalt(II) redox couple. When the metal center is quanti-
tatively either in the oxidized or in the reduced state, the deposit
exhibits much higher electrical resistance. The occurrence of
corrosion phenomena was also evident in the Nyquist diagrams
of the deposit obtained from CoTAPP solutions.
3.1.6. Electrochemical Quartz Crystal Microbalance
The Saurbrey [23] equation was used to evaluate the weight of
where f is the measured frequency variation, M is the mass
variation and q and q are density of the crystal and wave speed
Fig. 6. Suggested structure of the H2(t-o-NH2)PP polymer ®lm.
Table 1. Absorbances of the deposited ®lm on increasing the number of
potential sweeps. l  426 and 422 nm on ITO and gold-coated quartz,
accomplished in the conditions previously described. The whole
measurement system stops working just after the ®rst anodic
scan. The gold metal on the crystal quartz results in being cov-
ered by a dark-gold colored polymer ®lm: the electrical circuit
opens, so that no useful potential scan is possible anymore and
the measures from the frequency counter become completely
unreliable after a little while. These phenomena are completely
reproducible: the circuit opening can be ascribed to the growth of
a very low conducting ®lm on the electrode and the arising of
enormous noise in the response of the microwave counter is most
probably due to the high viscoelastic characteristics of the
polymer deposited, that should not have a well-packed structure,
apart from the rigidity resulting in uncontrolled energy dissipa-
tion within the ®lm [24]. On the other hand, as previously dis-
cussed, the very poor conducting character of the ®lm deposited
in the ®rst scan well accounts for the impossibility of any further
growth. Figure 10 shows the current=potential curve super-

3.1.7. Electropolymerization Mechanism
TAPP and the relevant metal derivatives polymerize through
the amino groups of the ortho-anilines, according to a radical
polymerization mechanism [13, 25]. The differences among the
voltammograms recorded on different electrode materials suggest
the occurrence of strong interactions between the resulting
polyporphyrins and the electrode surface. Moreover, since the
presence of chloride ions is necessary in order that polymeriza-
tion proceeds, these ions can be supposed to be selectively
incorporated into the positively charged polymer matrix during
the growth due to their suitable size, similarly to the behavior
reported for the electropolymerization of pyrrol [26, 27]. The fact
that ®lm formation takes place at the ®rst scan, and only at the
®rst scan, suggests that peaks A,C, and D, which are recorded in
the subsequent potential cycles (see Fig. 2), are not involved in
the polymerization process, that is supposed to occur at peaks G
and G0 in Figure 1. The poorly conducting character of the ®lm
can be ascribed to the fact that, due to the steric hindrance of the
ortho-amino groups, the four ortho-anilines are twisted out of the
porphyrin ring plane, so that the polymerization mechanism
makes the polymer not to grow in linear macromolecules. As
shown by the UV-visible spectra, planarity of the porphyrin ring
is retained, but heavy distortions are induced in the correspon-
dence to the bonds between the porphyrin ring and the anilines
(see Fig. 4): the lack of planarity [15], actually limited to rela-
tively small portions of the macromolecules, does not allow
effective extensive  electron conjugation, and gives reason for
the relatively low conductivity of the polymer. Chloride ions
could be located between two porphyrin planes, in a `sandwich'
conformation, or nearby, as counter ions of the positively charged
Finally, referring to Figure 2, peak A is attributable to the
overoxidation of the polymer system, implying irreversible
modi®cations of the structure. This process could affect both
polymeric bridges and porphyrin rings, which is consistent with
the formation of alkyl-pyrrole and of oxidized residues, like
quinole and ketone moieties, that should be responsible for
reduction peak C and directly associated oxidation peak D. These
peaks are also recorded when performing cyclic voltammetric
tests over the same potential range, in 1 M HCl solution, with a
Fig. 7. AFM image of a Pt surface covered partially with the polymeric porphyrin ®lm.

atoms, attacks the polymeric structure with formation of poly-

3.2. Potentiometric Measurements
Electrodes modi®ed with polymers similar to those studied by
us have been reported to show interesting capabilities as anionic
potentiometric sensors in previous works [7, 29, 30]. On this line,
we have tested a series of ®ve different coated platinum elec-
trodes, i.e., those obtained by polymerization of: unsubstituted
TAPP, FeTAPP, NiTAPP, CuTAPP, and CoTAPP with respect to
the following anions: Clÿ, Brÿ, Iÿ, SCNÿ, NOÿ
Furthermore, the electrodes with the coatings from unsubstituted
TAPP were also tested with respect to OHÿ, CH3COOÿ, SO2ÿ
anions and to Cu, Zn, and Fe cations.
The results reported below could only be obtained when the
electrode was polarized at positive potentials before use, inducing
a positive charge on the ®lm, which is compatible with the pre-
sence of chloride ions inside the structure. On the contrary, if the
®lm was reduced before the measurements by dipping into a 1 M
hydrazine methanol solution, meaningless potentiometric data
were collected. These results may be interpreted assuming that
the ®lm, when in the oxidized conductive form, acts as a redox
mediator with respect to the above listed anions. The redox
couples in which the anions are involved may be electro-
chemically irreversible and, moreover, they can be involved in
chemical interactions with the ®lm leading to modi®cations in the
polymeric surface. The potential actually recorded should hence
be due to the redox systems of the porphyrin ®lms. The number
of electrons determining the slopes of the calibration plots for the
anions can be different when measured on bare platinum or on
platinum covered with porphyrin ®lm.
Since the detected anions, according to the Hofmeister series
[31], have a low hydration energy, it seems likewise that they
should undergo dehydration in order to interact with the ®lm. On
the other hand, ®lms from metal-TAPP complexes led to different
behavior at varying the nature of the metal. This could be
explained admitting coordination of the anions in the axial

depending on both the metal and the ligand nature. As an

Moreover, the coatings from the Fe, Cu, and Co-TAPP complexes

Not all of the results obtained in the potentiometric tests can
always be given a rationale. The behavior of the electro-
chemically generated polyporphyrin modi®ed electrodes as
potentiometric sensors is a function of i) the oxidation state of the
polymer ®lm; ii) the presence or absence of a metal center inside
the ring and, as was evidenced by the last tests reported, iii) the
solution pH. Furthermore, the selectivity of the sensor is strictly
related to the exact conditions under which the electro-
polymerization was carried out (e.g., potentiostatic or potentio-
concentration of monomer in the solution). This could be
accounted for by the spongy structure hypothesized for the
polymer: on the one side it behaves like a sort of ``molecular
sieve'' with respect to the anions, and on the other side the anions
interact with the polymer according with their hydration energy
[30, 31], so that it is quite reasonable that the structural para-
meters of similar solids are strongly dependent on the exact
conditions of the crystallization process. The potentiometric

selectivity, of potentiometric devices in which a similar polymer
